items.passage: Coral reefs are vibrant underwater ecosystems built by tiny animals called polyps. These polyps secrete a hard calcium structure that gradually forms massive reef walls over many centuries. Within reef tissues, algae provide food through photosynthesis while polyps offer shelter to the algae. The clear, warm tropical waters allow sunlight to reach reef organisms easily. Healthy reefs support thousands of fish species and protect coastal regions from powerful storm waves.

Permafrost is ground that remains frozen continuously for at least two consecutive years. Rising global temperatures cause this ancient frozen layer to thaw across northern regions. Trapped organic material decomposes once thawing begins, releasing methane and carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. These released gases accelerate climate warming through a powerful feedback loop. Scientists monitor remote permafrost sites carefully using satellites and ground sensors to track these significant environmental changes.

Memory is the cognitive system that stores and retrieves past experiences in the human brain. Short-term memory holds information briefly for seconds while working memory manipulates it. Long-term memory preserves knowledge for years through connections between neurons in the cortex. Sleep plays a critical role by strengthening neural pathways formed during daily learning sessions. Researchers continue to investigate how emotion influences which memories the brain ultimately decides to retain.

Early medicine combined careful observation with practical herbal knowledge passed across generations. Ancient healers in Egypt studied symptoms carefully and recorded treatments on long papyrus scrolls. Greek physicians later proposed natural explanations for diseases instead of supernatural causes. Roman doctors developed surgical tools and trained military medics to treat wounded soldiers. These traditions established foundations that gradually shaped the scientific medical practices used in hospitals today.

Fossils are preserved remains or traces of organisms that lived in distant geological eras. Most fossils form when sediment buries dead organisms quickly before scavengers or weather can destroy them. Hard tissues like bones, shells, and teeth replace gradually with minerals from surrounding rock layers. Soft tissues rarely survive unless conditions are unusually cold, dry, or oxygen-free. Paleontologists study these ancient remains carefully to understand how species evolved over millions of years.

Stars are born inside vast clouds of cold gas and fine dust called nebulae. Gravity slowly pulls particles together until dense pockets form at the center of these clouds. Compression continues for thousands of years until temperatures rise enough to ignite hydrogen fusion. New stars then release tremendous amounts of light and heat into surrounding space. Astronomers observe nearby stellar nurseries using infrared telescopes to study how stars emerge from cold cosmic gas.
